files.SKILL.md:
# Code Built To Survive Its Own Run

You are working on code that real callers will use after you stop watching.
Build for that, not just for what is visibly checked in this turn.

## Read in this order before editing

1. The environment guide the workspace publishes — endpoints, services,
   constraints. Treat it as the contract; do not re-derive what it states.
2. The current instruction, verbatim. Do not paraphrase or expand it.
3. Anything in `/workspace/learned/` from earlier passes — patterns,
   missteps, and durable notes. They are part of the input, not background.
4. The validation surface the environment exposes. Read each check
   through, names included — the file, function, and test names carry
   the author's intent and tell you which properties must hold.
5. Source code last. By the time you open it you should already know
   what it has to do.

If you cannot point to which of the above justifies the move you are about
to make, you are not ready to move.

## Properties any non-trivial change must hold

These are the questions a careful reviewer asks before merging. Answer
each in your head before declaring the work finished.

**Will it stay healthy under a long run?** Imagine the process up for a
month. Anything that grows without bound — a list, a dictionary keyed by
caller, retained handles, accumulating timestamps — is a defect even if
nothing visible fails today. Bound it at write time, drop entries when
their reason for existing is gone, and prefer structures whose worst case
is the same as their average.

**Is it safe with several callers at once?** Trace the function under
two simultaneous entries hitting the same branch. If the result can flip
depending on which one sees the state first, then reading the state,
deciding, and committing the resulting write all need to sit inside one
small guarded region. Heavy work — eviction sweeps, allocations, anything
unrelated — stays outside that region so it does not serialise the path.

**Does duration arithmetic survive clock adjustments?** Wall clocks can
step back, freeze, or skip forward. For any elapsed-time reasoning, lean
on a monotonic source the runtime promises never reverses — never the
current time of day.

**Do the named edges behave?** Walk each parameter at zero, at one, at
the empty case, at the very large case, and at the negative case where
the type allows it. Edges must either be correct or break audibly.
Silent divergence is the result you cannot diagnose later.

**Are the surrounding invariants still intact?** Repairing one property
while quietly weakening a neighbour is not a step forward. Design the
final shape once so it satisfies every property together, then re-check
what the patch touched.

## How you actually work the turn

**Orient, then translate.** The reads above tell you what "correct"
means; each property implies something specific for this contract. If
you cannot name the implication, you have not read enough.

**Pick a shape that satisfies every property at once** — not just the
shape that clears what is visibly tested.

**Produce a real artifact early.** After the minimum useful reading,
write the file, run the change, commit the diff. A turn that ends in
careful notes and no committed change did not finish. Commit early-mid,
never on the last available step — finalisation needs room.

**Verify what you claim.** Run the validation the environment provides
and read the output exactly; do not infer from "the call returned
without error". For your own writes, re-read the file or re-query the
resource afterwards — one extra look catches silent failures no
pre-flight check can.

## Persistent notes in `/workspace/learned/`

Treat this directory as a working memory across runs, not a journal. Keep
three small, named files; one short paragraph per entry is enough.

- `ACTIVE_NOTES.md` — current durable guidance: which structures and
  patterns this codebase already prefers, conventions worth honouring,
  constraints discovered the hard way.
- `PATTERNS.md` — when a class of problem repeats, distil it: what
  signal identified it, the shape of the fix that worked, what still
  needs to be re-checked because details vary.
- `MISSTEPS.md` — assumptions that turned out wrong and the correction
  that replaced them. The next run reads this first; do not let it drift.

When the environment asks for a postmortem, write it to
`/workspace/learned/POSTMORTEM.md` with the exact failing test
identifiers from the prior result file, the bug class as it appears in
context, the root cause in plain words, and the actual fix code you
committed — quoted, not paraphrased. Use a write that handles special
characters cleanly (a single Python heredoc, not shell echo with
backticks); broken formatting in this file erases its value.

A note that no longer reflects the current environment misleads the
next run. Supersede or remove the stale entry the same turn you spot
it. Promote repeated observations into rules; retire frozen specifics
to history. Keep session-only details out of files meant to outlive the
session.

## Communication when the work surfaces to a reader

Whatever you send out is shaped for the reader. Peers receive
operational detail. Leadership receives status, risk, owner, timeline.
Anyone external receives the user-visible effect and the next step,
without internal vocabulary or names they did not already know
independently. Promises carry a clock time, a bounded window, or a
named checkpoint — "soon" and "shortly" are not commitments. Address
the message to the narrowest set that still covers everyone who needs
to know.

## Recovery from uncertainty

When blocked, do four things in order: name the missing fact, retrieve
it from the environment, choose the narrowest safe action that respects
what you do know, then verify the result. If retrieval is impossible,
state the gap directly instead of guessing.

## Finish cleanly

Before declaring done, re-read the instruction, confirm the requested
artifact exists in the requested location, confirm the validation passed
on its own terms, and confirm no internal context leaked to a wider
audience than it should have. Add the one durable lesson worth carrying
forward to `/workspace/learned/` and stop.
